sphere_commentary.overview: Across 997 responses spanning 30 protocols, the aggregate signal is cautiously positive: series means cluster between 6.36 and 6.78, with standard deviations in the 1.4–1.9 range suggesting meaningful but not excessive population variance. The framework is performing within expected parameters for an early-stage independent research project — no protocol is signalling systematic harm, and positive-response rates (pct_pos) broadly track mean scores, which is an internal consistency check worth noting. Sample sizes remain the primary constraint on interpretation: only 8 protocols have reached 'meaningful' confidence (n≥50), and no protocol has crossed n=100, so all pattern-level conclusions should be held lightly.
sphere_commentary.cross_series: HEALING leads on both volume (n=581) and mean score (6.78), which is consistent with an established audience finding entry points through high-frequency use cases like anxiety, sleep, and over-stimulation. THRIVING shows a tighter standard deviation (1.56 vs 1.77 for HEALING) suggesting a more homogeneous respondent population, possibly self-selecting optimisers rather than crisis-adjacent users — this is worth tracking as n grows. TRANSFORMING carries the lowest mean (6.36) and meaningful variability, which is structurally expected given that ASCENT protocols target low-affect states where baseline scoring is suppressed and response curves are less predictable; this should not be read as underperformance.
sphere_commentary.anomalies: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10) flags as the most statistically unusual protocol in the dataset: mean 5.6 with std 2.2 against a distribution of [0,0,1,1,2,1,0,3,1,1,0] that shows a pronounced gap at score 6 with mass at both low (3–5) and high (7–9) ends — a tentative bimodal signature. At n=10 this cannot be confirmed as a real subgroup effect, but it warrants close monitoring as the primary anomaly flag. X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=11) shows std=2.31, the highest in the dataset, with a similarly fragmented distribution [0,0,0,1,2,0,2,0,2,3,1] — extreme spread for an ASCENT protocol targeting motivation, consistent with either population mismatch or high sensitivity to pre-session state. T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15) shows a strikingly compressed distribution with 9 of 15 responses at score 7 and zeros at 8 and 9 — this spike pattern is unusual and could indicate a ceiling-effect artefact, a very specific subpopulation, or a rating anchoring behaviour in early respondents. T-FC-L3 (Summit) shows the lowest pct_pos (45%) in THRIVING at n=22, with no scores above 8 — a soft signal that the L3 routing for Focus and Clarity may not be serving the respondents who reach it.
sphere_commentary.development_signals: 1. T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, pct_pos=45%): This is the weakest signal in the THRIVING series and the only THRIVING protocol where pct_pos drops below 50%. The distribution shows scores compressed between 6–7 with no high-end responses, and it follows T-FC-L2 (Waypoint) which scores well (mean=7.06, pct_pos=65%). This inversion — where L3 underperforms L2 — warrants investigation of Adviser routing logic: are listeners being escalated to Summit prematurely, or does the protocol's HOLD architecture need a longer entrainment ramp for the population arriving at L3? Suggested action: audit the routing criteria between Waypoint and Summit, and consider whether Summit requires a protocol revision to extend its hold phase or introduce a more gradual phi-sigmoid onset. 2. X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.6, std=2.2): The tentative bimodal distribution and highest variance-adjusted concern in the TRANSFORMING series suggests this protocol may be landing very differently across subpopulations — one group responding well, another not engaging. At n=10 no action should be taken on the protocol itself, but suggested action is to implement a targeted data collection push for Cosmos specifically, including optional post-session qualitative prompts, to determine whether the split correlates with a listener characteristic (e.g. caregiver vs direct listener, clinical vs self-referred). 3. X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.0, pct_pos=36%) and X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=11, std=2.31): The Motivation and Drive sub-series shows the weakest aggregate performance in TRANSFORMING, with Trail holding the lowest pct_pos in the entire dataset at 36%. ASCENT protocols are structurally challenging, but the combination of low positive response and high variance across L2 and L3 suggests the sub-series may benefit from a dedicated review once n reaches 30 per level. Suggested action: flag X-MD as a priority data collection target for the next review cycle; consider whether the ASCENT arc across Trail and Switchback is appropriately calibrated to the wing ratio, and whether a bridging variant between Footpath and Trail is warranted. 4. H-SL-L2 (Hammock, n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.44) and H-SL-L3 (Midnight, n=33, mean=7.03, std=1.55): The Sleep sub-series shows the most consistent performance in the dataset — both L2 and L3 have means above 7.0, low standard deviations relative to series norms, and pct_pos above 64%. This is the strongest candidate for a VOL002 or extended-format development. Suggested action: prioritise Hammock and Midnight for a second-volume development pass, and consider whether a dedicated sleep maintenance protocol (HOLD type) could complement the existing DESCENT architecture to serve listeners who fall asleep successfully but experience early waking.

by_protocol.H-AX-L1: Meadow is the highest-mean protocol in the Anxiety and Stress sub-series (mean=7.14, n=65) and one of the stronger performers in HEALING overall, though its std=1.88 indicates notable spread — the distribution shows a concentration in the 6–9 range with a modest tail at lower scores, consistent with a diverse anxiety-presenting population finding variable but generally positive utility.
by_protocol.H-AX-L2: Cottage (mean=6.9, n=51) shows a cleaner distribution than L1, with 67% positive response and a concentration in the 7–8 range; the tighter spread relative to Meadow is consistent with a more self-selected population reaching L2, and the signal here is stable and encouraging.
by_protocol.H-AX-L3: Valley (mean=6.97, n=38, developing confidence) continues the sub-series pattern of solid performance; the distribution is slightly more dispersed than Cottage but pct_pos holds at 61%, suggesting L3 listeners are still engaging productively with the longer entrainment arc.
by_protocol.H-CR-L1: Anchor (mean=6.35, n=48) is performing within the structurally expected range for a crisis-oriented DESCENT protocol — listeners arriving in acute distress bring suppressed baseline scores, and a mean above 6 with pct_pos at 52% should be read as a meaningful outcome signal, not a weakness.
by_protocol.H-CR-L2: Harbor (mean=6.14, n=35) holds the second-lowest mean in the HEALING series, but this is consistent with the acute distress framing; the distribution clusters in the 5–8 range with limited extreme-low responses, suggesting the protocol is not aversive — it is working within a compressed response window typical of crisis populations.
by_protocol.H-CR-L3: Horizon (mean=6.31, n=29) shows a slight recovery over Harbor (6.14) which is a mildly interesting inversion worth monitoring — one hypothesis is that the longer L3 format provides more entrainment time for the most dysregulated listeners, but n is too small to support this interpretation with confidence.
by_protocol.H-OS-L1: Pebble (mean=6.54, n=72) is the highest-n protocol in the dataset and shows a broad distribution with a mode at score 6 and a long tail into the 7–9 range; pct_pos at 51% is the lowest in the Over-stimulation sub-series, suggesting L1 may be catching a heterogeneous population that includes both mild and severe over-stimulation presentations — a routing or intake framing adjustment could potentially improve fit.
by_protocol.H-OS-L2: Rainfall (mean=6.71, n=58) improves on Pebble across all metrics — higher mean, similar std, and pct_pos jumping to 62%; this is consistent with L2 self-selection filtering toward listeners who found L1 partially effective and are seeking deeper entrainment.
by_protocol.H-OS-L3: Glacier (mean=6.98, n=44) is the strongest performer in the Over-stimulation sub-series and shows a well-formed distribution concentrated in the 6–9 range; the progressive improvement across L1–L3 in this sub-series is one of the cleaner level-progression signals in the dataset.
by_protocol.H-SL-L1: Lantern (mean=6.97, n=61) anchors a consistently high-performing sleep sub-series; its distribution is notably flat across the 5–9 range which indicates population diversity in sleep difficulty severity, but the aggregate signal is strong.
by_protocol.H-SL-L2: Hammock (mean=7.04, n=47) has the lowest standard deviation in the HEALING series (1.44) and one of the highest pct_pos values at 68%; this tightness suggests a well-matched population-protocol pairing and makes Hammock a candidate for extended development consideration.
by_protocol.H-SL-L3: Midnight (mean=7.03, n=33) maintains L2-level performance at L3, which is unusual in a positive direction — most sub-series show some regression at L3 due to smaller and more varied populations, but the sleep sub-series appears to sustain engagement across the full progression.
by_protocol.T-CF-L1: Ember (mean=6.66, n=38) shows a moderate signal for Creative Flow at L1, with pct_pos at 53% indicating the population is split roughly evenly at the positive threshold; the distribution is reasonable but unremarkable, and more data would help clarify whether this reflects a true population average or early-adopter variance.
by_protocol.T-CF-L2: Current (mean=6.58, n=24) shows a compressed distribution (std=1.26) concentrated in the 6–8 range; the slightly lower mean than L1 is not a concern at this sample size, and the tighter spread suggests a more homogeneous population reaching L2.
by_protocol.T-CF-L3: Aurora (mean=7.17, n=18, early data) is the highest mean in the Creative Flow sub-series and shows a clean concentration in the 6–8 range with no low-end responses; this is an encouraging early signal but should be held loosely given n=18 — the absence of low scores may reflect selection effects rather than protocol performance.
by_protocol.T-FC-L1: Compass (mean=6.4, n=42) is the baseline for Focus and Clarity; the distribution is broad with a mode at score 7 but significant mass in the 4–6 range, and pct_pos at 55% suggests moderate fit — this is a functioning but not optimised L1 entry point.
by_protocol.T-FC-L2: Waypoint (mean=7.06, n=31) is a notable improvement over Compass and one of the stronger THRIVING signals — the distribution is concentrated in the 6–9 range with minimal low-end mass, and the L1-to-L2 improvement (6.4 to 7.06) is the largest positive level-progression jump in the THRIVING series.
by_protocol.T-FC-L3: Summit (mean=6.23, n=22, pct_pos=45%) is a development concern: it underperforms both Compass and Waypoint, has the lowest pct_pos in THRIVING, and shows no scores above 8 — the L2-to-L3 regression warrants routing and protocol review as a priority action.
by_protocol.T-MB-L1: Stone (mean=7.17, n=29) is the highest mean in the Maintenance and Balance sub-series and one of the top performers in THRIVING, with pct_pos at 69% and a clean distribution concentrated in the 7–9 range; the low std (1.29) suggests good population-protocol fit.
by_protocol.T-MB-L2: Axis (mean=6.59, n=22) shows a regression from Stone (7.17 to 6.59) with notably higher variance (std=1.92); the distribution is more dispersed with low-end responses appearing, which may reflect a more heterogeneous population at L2 or a protocol calibration issue — worth monitoring as n grows.
by_protocol.T-MB-L3: Orbit (mean=6.87, n=15, early data) shows a distribution spike with 9 of 15 responses at score 7 and zeros at adjacent scores 8 and 9, which is an unusual concentration pattern; this could reflect a rating anchoring artefact in a small early cohort and should be monitored closely rather than interpreted structurally at this stage.
by_protocol.X-MC-L1: Cavern (mean=6.62, n=24) is a reasonable baseline for the Mind and Clarity sub-series within TRANSFORMING; pct_pos at 50% is exactly at threshold, and the distribution is moderately concentrated in the 6–8 range — a functional but not strong signal.
by_protocol.X-MC-L2: Starlight (mean=6.59, n=17, early data) holds close to L1 performance with a cleaner distribution (std=1.33); the absence of extreme scores in either direction suggests a consistent if modest response — interpretation should wait for n to develop.
by_protocol.X-MC-L3: Cosmos (mean=5.6, n=10, early data) is the primary anomaly flag in the dataset: the lowest mean across all protocols with std=2.2 and a distribution that suggests a tentative bimodal pattern; this cannot be confirmed at n=10 but warrants prioritised data collection and qualitative investigation.
by_protocol.X-MD-L1: Footpath (mean=6.24, n=21) is a low-but-functional baseline for Motivation and Drive, with pct_pos at 43% — below threshold but within the expected range for an ASCENT protocol targeting low-affect states where the scoring baseline is structurally suppressed.
by_protocol.X-MD-L2: Trail (mean=6.0, n=14, early data) holds the lowest pct_pos in the entire dataset at 36%; while ASCENT protocols are expected to score lower, this figure warrants attention — at n=14 no protocol action is appropriate, but Trail should be a priority data collection target.
by_protocol.X-MD-L3: Switchback (mean=6.91, n=11, early data) shows the highest standard deviation in the dataset at 2.31, with a fragmented distribution [0,0,0,1,2,0,2,0,2,3,1] that suggests extreme response heterogeneity; the mean appears relatively high given the variance, and this protocol requires substantially more data before any signal can be trusted.
by_protocol.X-ME-L1: Breeze (mean=6.29, n=35) anchors the Mood and Energy sub-series with a moderate signal; pct_pos at 43% is below threshold, consistent with ASCENT-type protocols, and the distribution shows a mode at score 6 with gradual tailing — a plausible baseline for a low-affect entry point.
by_protocol.X-ME-L2: Sunrise (mean=6.48, n=25) shows improvement over Breeze in both mean and pct_pos (43% to 60%), which is an encouraging level-progression signal for the Mood and Energy sub-series; the distribution is concentrated in the 6–8 range with limited extreme-low responses.
by_protocol.X-ME-L3: Updraft (mean=6.28, n=18, early data) shows a slight regression from Sunrise (6.48 to 6.28) with similar variance; at n=18 this is not interpretable as a real pattern, but it is worth monitoring to see whether the L2-to-L3 improvement trajectory holds as data accumulates.

H-SL-L1.sphere: Lantern (mean=6.97, n=61) anchors a consistently high-performing sleep sub-series; its distribution is notably flat across the 5–9 range which indicates population diversity in sleep difficulty severity, but the aggregate signal is strong.
